item.title: What we’re doing to these people is inhumane
item.content:encoded:
I seriously need to read an account of the Serpent’s Hand freeing these girls and their guy from the oppressive thumb of the SCP Foundation.
Chaotic good needs to win against lawful evil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Easy answers, as they both have the same answer:

The catgirls & wizard are primarily a political threat to normality. Their augmentations aren't out of reach for the Foundation as I've portrayed it in my other writing, but they (the BRC) have devoted all of their time, resources, and effort to overthrowing the status quo that the Foundation seeks to maintain. They're radical anarchists.

The Foundation could try to recruit them (I have gotten this question multiple times over the years and I am not sure why), but all they would end up with would be a quintet of non-cooperative, openly rebellious, cyberwared-up and very angry women actively attempting to sabotage them at every opportunity. Could you imagine them trying to wrangle Nanami (A-4) into following orders?

There's no ideological overlap to motivate them to cooperate, financial offerings wouldn't have much effect (since their MO is theft anyway) and coercion would just make them try harder to cause damage in retaliation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really new here… Please bear with me if this is a dumb question. My biggest takeaway after reading this was — why exactly are these being contained? The space wizard has an unusual disease and there's genetic modification and some cybernetics. Does that really rise to the level of concern for the foundation?

As a secondary note — the foundation has made use of augmented and anomalous individuals in task forces. Why isn't that on the table here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this was a truly unique and (imo) charming read. and a breath of fresh air. I'm looking forward to checking out the stealing solidarity hub… the box soliloquy made me sad. I want the wizard and the girls to escape and make awesomesauce vigilante mischief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait a minute, was this whole SCP a cunningly disguised reference to this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2085: This? Easy. It’s a symbol. Super-hero thing. It helps people believe easier, because symbols and icons are easier to believe in than people, a lot of the time. The world doesn’t like letting people believe in people, so they have to make do with symbols.

Like, just look at a bunch of kids playing pretend on the playground. They see the world, look out at it, and then go ‘No, fuck you world, I’m going to make something better, I’m going to be something better.’ They aren't going to laugh at the space wizards and cyborg catgirls, because they want to be the space wizards and cyborg catgirls. They're going to build spaceships and go to the moon and let nothing get in their way. Then they grow up, and find out that the world is built on broken dreams. They give their dreams to the world to find a place in the machine and the world tells them to build a box. They stick themselves in the box and sit there until they die, and the boxes go on until the universe ends. That's the box the girls were fighting against. That's the box I'm fighting against. We're going to be what the rest of the world could be, do what the rest of the world could do, take all those dreams and bring them back. If we need to get our hands dirty, so be it. The world is dirty and we have to deal with it until we can make something better. We're going to remind the world what it's like to not live inside a box.

I think I just found Space Wizard's theme song.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is all about catgirls, cyborgs, and space wizard, with anime memes.

What's not to love? It's perfection.

Joke aside, I love the story. I'm ALWAYS down for article with stories to tell, when it's engaging, and even better, when the author KNOW what kind of impression and emotions it wants to deliver. We know the Foundation didn't see them as something special, BUT you makes THE reader loves them by showing their personality, and makes that hit really hard.

I never successfully pulled a "humanoid SCP with anime reference" yet, so a big respect from me.

+1 AND added to my personal fave SCP pages!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cat girls? Nobody tell bright something tells me he'd be into that shit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry but I can't bring myself to like this. As an anime fan, even I thought the references were phoned in and ridiculous, including the Dio part and naming the girls after anime characters. (Why couldn't you just give them original names or at least codenames?) They just seem like on-note DeviantArt OCs to me. The only interesting part was Wizard and his cancer and that wasn't developed nearly enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The best SCPs are the ones with a story to tell, this goes above and beyond.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to head to an alternate universe where this is SCP-2000. A lot of people are downvoting this because it sounds "weeby" (The term which is heavily misinterpreted since it means someone who misunderstands Japanese culture and etiquette) but heck, there aren't many articles based on 90s anime which actually succeed. +9001

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor things

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i cant think of a good comment to write here but this SCP is really appealing
i love these SCPs that are just people, albeit weird people but they're still just people who didn't really do anything wrong
also fuckin love catgirls
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This may sound weird, but I like this SCP so much because it makes me appreciate the "box" so much more. It's a fantastically written SCP about how the foundation still has to carry out its duty and contain anomalies, even those that are zany and whimsical and generally good-hearted.

The best part of the entire SCP, in my opinion, is the note. It managed to make me feel sympathy for the catgirls and a little indignance at them accusing the foundation of being evil for trapping them despite not knowing that they simply have to be trapped until the end of time. Obviously, we know better, and that just makes the whole thing that much more notable.

It makes me kinda want to read more about the characters and what they do as they continue to stay in foundation custody. I never really got into the whole "stealing solidarity" canon.

Regarding the box analogy, I don't think it's right, from a pragmatic and moral standpoint, to figure out how to try and break out of the box. Instead, it would be better to try and figure out how to make the box larger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cynicism is for losers.
Laugh and spite the devil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not withdrawing my upvote here, but I will say that I have had to think twice about it; time has made me more cynical, perhaps too much so. I no longer think there's any real room for starry-eyed wonder in a world that also contains Procedure 110-Montauk… especially if nothing less than the world's continued existence depends on it. Once you've read the dossier on 231 — and especially when you become cynical enough to read a relevant metaphor into it — it's hard to take the funny, whimsical, or defiantly optimistic skips in the spirit in which they're meant. Hard to laugh, or to smile at whimsy, when you remember that this is the same universe in which we have to regularly commit unspeakable atrocities on a young girl just to keep the world's population alive a few more days. In real life, as in the SCP 'verse, the very foundations of civilization are grounded on unmerited suffering.

Omelas reigns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for being well-written AND the JoJo reference

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's fair enough, it is pretty interesting to think about the perspective of someone whose first impression of the Foundation is being contained by them.

We really do need more toast-related employees around here

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
True, but this is because you say it as a Foundation person. See it as an amazing story instead. Also read the hub for it. I linked it in an above post.

I am saying this, Toaster to Toasty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
PSST HEY. THIS STUFF IS GOOD IF YOU READ THE HUB ON THEM

TAKE THIS AND READ FOR THIS TO BE EPICNESS.

THIS IS THE BEAUTY OF IT THAT YOU MISSED. THEY MOVED IT

And when you get to it, her is music for phase 4

EPIC MUSIC

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehhhhh.

It's a really good article overall, I don't think that needs to be said again. But personally it just rubs me the wrong way. I've always seen the Foundation as being a really good example of 'ends justify the means'; they're definitely cold and harsh, but they're the ones stopping the world from being overrun by stuff like 096 and 682. Maybe it's just me, but the whole attitude they seem to have toward the Foundation in this article comes off as a little holier-than-thou. I might be focusing on the wrong part of the article there, but that just stood out to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Stand User: Major Motoko Kusanagi (pseudonym)
Stand Name:「Vapor Trail」
Description: A humanoid clad in a robe, wizard hat, and advanced crew escape suit. 「Vapor Trail」will deploy aerosolized nanomachines that self-replicate to break down structures that hold it or its user. It is a wholly autonomous stand, albeit very loyal to its user.
Stats:

  • Power: A
  • Speed: C
  • Range: B
  • Durability: D
  • Precision: E
  • Potential: B

… you should also update the footnote describing their name sources to include JJBA.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After almost three years, I realized that there was a great error I had not addressed, and that I must make immediate edits to amend this grievous moral folly.

The situation has been rectified. You may go about your business.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I fell in love with SCP-2085-A-4. +1 for japanese cyborg catgirls

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes me want to try playing EP out of sudden

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I allways enjoy cyborg themed SCPs.
Upvoted for the Ghost in the Shell reference and Interview 2085-A-4.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry but I don't, in a clear mind, see how I can bring myself to upvote this article.

I did read through the posts you left and yes, I do (or so I think) see what you've been trying here; as an Asian whose childhood has been literally jam-packed with many Animes I sensed it right away after spotting the cat ear description, but that just was not enough. While I recognize your effort in creating this article, I fear much of it was wasted away in meaningless scripts and descriptions. For example I don't really see the purpose of interview scripts from 2085-As; are they there to add humor? are they there to hint about "Black Rabbit Company", because I think there are hardly any more clues than the interview with 2085 himself. Yes it shows their unique attitudes toward the Foundation and shows they care about Wizard in their own ways, but do we really need five different interview scripts to explain it?

Maybe I'm just mad after reading a long article and still finding myself wondering about what's really going on - admittedly it happens more often than you think for me - and judging from the general response I assume it's possible that I've failed to find true beauty of the scip, but honestly, that's how I feel inside, and I doubt it will change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks a LOT :D

item.content:encoded: take my feels you fiend +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved it. But even if it weren't cool by itself…

I’m an average guy from an average background. If Joe Everyman and John Doe were a gay couple who adopted a kid, I would be the next door neighbor who doesn’t even get a mention in the newspaper.

That line alone deserves a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's what I figured.
But can I just say, I love this article, I love Stealing Solidarity, and most of all, I love you.
You're honestly one of my favorite writers on this site.
Mad props, bro.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:

Pull your mind out from the gutter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole time I was reading this, I was thinking, "Jesus Christ, somebody's Shadowrun campaign leaked into the real world and these are the PCs who got out." +1

item.title: There is a story.
item.content:encoded:
Loads of people say throughout the comments that there should be a story of them breaking out.
There is.
http://www.scp-wiki.net/stealingsolidarityhub

Anyway, I really like this SCP, just because it breaks the normal rules and still works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh, no. This was on the site for, like, over a year before Solidarity was even written. This is popular because they're sympathetic and well-written characters. A lot of people have downvoted this. Upvotes just outnumber the downvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The main reason (in my opinon) was the Stealing Solidarity series of stories, which add a ton of backstory, and is one of my personal favorite hubs on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly don't see why so many people like this so much. I find it bad for all of the obvious reasons, and the attempted hook is something that has been done before many times, and better at that. The cancer thing actually might be interesting if it wasn't for the over-the-top cheesy explanation given for it, which only serves to reinforce the sententious message.

item.title: Sad little nerd that I am!
item.content:encoded:
Oh it's so sad that I recognized nearly all the references lol.

I love this for keeping diversity on the site. Some lightheartedness without being TOO out there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know… I don't like this in my headcanon. And I can't really see this fitting in any SCP-verse. But there is damn good plot here. This works better as a standalone series without SCP. I'll be reading Stealing Solidarity directly after this just to continue this little adventure.

That said, I wish more articles had these kind of enthusiastic story elements. Doesn't really care whether you like this or if it fits into your canon. It just fucking is, so deal with it. It's bold, it's fresh, and it's funny.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I said, get my goddamn dick in your goddamn mouth and suck it.

Wait.
Wait.
Are you implying that SCP-2085-A-4 is a hermaphrodite?
Dammit djoric stop pandering to my futa fetish

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No need for an alternate universe: that's basically what happened.

The tech for growing people is relatively well established in the Djoricverse - the Foundation uses it pretty significantly, as do some smaller-scale, even more shady places. The place the girls came from was more of a lab than a factory, and offered a variety of models to various underworld buyers. Not Marshall Carter and Dark, but the same business practices and clientele.

The process is not as easily done as the Foundation makes it seem: the girls were originally part of a batch of 60. 18 managed to survive the accelerated growth process. Suicide and burnout rates were overall very high, especially with some of the other model lines.

The girls did trash the lab and kill as many of the producers as they could, though a few of the managers survived, and some of their fellow vat-grown siblings might still be under their control.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanna see that anime, and yeah pretty much I could share that headcannon with you, there are even tales that say that all scp are manufactured by the O5 or the foundators of the SCP foundation, they even mentioned able, iris, 231 etc… as humans that they knew they where modifing I think it its in one of the SCP 001 proposals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Headcanon time!

Somewhere in the Foundation Multiverse, there's a factory (though probably not THE Factory) that manufactures made-to-order humans. The Foundation may or may not get D-Class from them. They have mindless anatomically accurate test subjects; dumb, barely sentient laborers; soldiers and bodyguards of varying degrees; and even doctors and engineers. The catgirls are part of a batch that was meant to be high-ranking members of an eccentric's personal guard detail, but something mucked up the production, leaving these four alive and free and their sisters dead. They may or may not blow up or have already blown up their birthplace… They're basically anime, furry, female versions of the protagonist from the Hitman film.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You people can whack me with a big ass hammer or whatever if i say that this is slighty anime-esque or reminiscent of some anime, yet the execution, character personality, background and almost everything else here proves that there are not bad ideas, just poor executions, thus +1

No i dont hate anime, i just hate when people over uses it, or when they use it badly and this is not the case for me.

Edit: readed the rest of the comments gotta try drawning our space wizard friend, still no rifles, it kinda gives me now the feel of "charlie angels" not seeing him anywhere but only those 5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No assault rifles or space wizard, 0/10.

I like the level of detail that went into this. The kind of spacesuit is even better than the Apollo-era stuff I'd initially imagined in my head. Bravo, sir. On a sad note, I find A-3 to be very naïve. I can't see them ending their lives in containment or free; they're gonna have some sort of last stand, and it will be tragic.

Anyways, I like this, so upboats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this was drawn by reddit user jenssosaurus, who is am immensely awesome human being who should feel good about themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fucking amazing as always, Djoric. I would love to see a Tale centered around this gang of misfits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, for one, would love to see a tale based around this skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gah!

Djoric, this is the most inappropriate, ill advised, unfitting SCP I have ever seen! This is exactly the sort of anime fanfic mary sue bullshit we are supposed to be avoiding. It utterly destroys any semblance to the style and tone this site is known for. I am so peeved by this SCP that after 6 years spent lurking this site on and off this thing was what made me make an account. An account made purely so I could tell you just how utterly weird and out of place it is!

And yet…

And yet I absolutely love it! It's monstrously well written. It's terribly imaginative. It's horrifically thought provoking. It is everything a good SCP should be and more. The only way it could be better is if Wizard actually brake the gang out of containment though some stupidly convoluted manner. GAH! Just take my upvote! Take it and stop ruining my mind!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha-ha, 101! Thanks, everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Djoric, if your other articles didn't already make me fall in love with your work, this one would.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bravo +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sticking with my shattered realities headcanon, I love that these characters exist. I love that they fight crime and bad guys. I love that the Foundation has locked them up. I love that there's a conflict of interest, of if they should or shouldn't be locked up, type struggle in here.
